content.code:
local variable value: 8
global variable value: 5

content.text:
This code prints:local variable value: 8
global variable value: 4Avoid variable shadowingShadowing of local variables should generally be avoided, as it can lead to inadvertent errors where the wrong variable is used or modified. Some compilers will issue a warning when a variable is shadowed.For the same reason that we recommend avoiding shadowing local variables, we recommend avoiding shadowing global variables as well. This is trivially avoidable if all of your global names use a “g_” prefix.Best practiceAvoid variable shadowing.For gcc usersGCC and Clang support the flag-Wshadowthat will generate warnings if a variable is shadowed. There are several subvariants of this flag (-Wshadow=global,-Wshadow=local, and-Wshadow=compatible-local. Consult theGCC documentationfor an explanation of the differences.Visual Studio has such warnings enabled by default.Next lesson7.6Internal linkageBack to table of contentsPrevious lesson7.4Introduction to global variables

Shadowing of local variables should generally be avoided, as it can lead to inadvertent errors where the wrong variable is used or modified. Some compilers will issue a warning when a variable is shadowed.For the same reason that we recommend avoiding shadowing local variables, we recommend avoiding shadowing global variables as well. This is trivially avoidable if all of your global names use a “g_” prefix.Best practiceAvoid variable shadowing.For gcc usersGCC and Clang support the flag-Wshadowthat will generate warnings if a variable is shadowed. There are several subvariants of this flag (-Wshadow=global,-Wshadow=local, and-Wshadow=compatible-local. Consult theGCC documentationfor an explanation of the differences.Visual Studio has such warnings enabled by default.Next lesson7.6Internal linkageBack to table of contentsPrevious lesson7.4Introduction to global variables

For the same reason that we recommend avoiding shadowing local variables, we recommend avoiding shadowing global variables as well. This is trivially avoidable if all of your global names use a “g_” prefix.Best practiceAvoid variable shadowing.For gcc usersGCC and Clang support the flag-Wshadowthat will generate warnings if a variable is shadowed. There are several subvariants of this flag (-Wshadow=global,-Wshadow=local, and-Wshadow=compatible-local. Consult theGCC documentationfor an explanation of the differences.Visual Studio has such warnings enabled by default.Next lesson7.6Internal linkageBack to table of contentsPrevious lesson7.4Introduction to global variables
Best practiceAvoid variable shadowing.
Avoid variable shadowing.

For gcc usersGCC and Clang support the flag-Wshadowthat will generate warnings if a variable is shadowed. There are several subvariants of this flag (-Wshadow=global,-Wshadow=local, and-Wshadow=compatible-local. Consult theGCC documentationfor an explanation of the differences.Visual Studio has such warnings enabled by default.

GCC and Clang support the flag-Wshadowthat will generate warnings if a variable is shadowed. There are several subvariants of this flag (-Wshadow=global,-Wshadow=local, and-Wshadow=compatible-local. Consult theGCC documentationfor an explanation of the differences.Visual Studio has such warnings enabled by default.
Visual Studio has such warnings enabled by default.
